coredata.dc:title: Confined construction of CDs@MIL-101 as reusable and turn-on fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive water detection based on adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism

coredata.dc:description: Fabrication a reusable and highly sensitive fluorescence sensor in a controllable manner for water detection in solvents and humidity remains a top priority target yet a challenging task. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) are successfully implanted into confined metal–organic framework MIL-101(Cr) cages via “ship in the bottle” strategy and the confined CDs are firstly observed in MIL-101(Cr) cage showing high dispersion and high N content by advanced iDPC STEM technique. The highly selective water capture and sensitive changes of the fluorescence property with solution polarity and hydrogen-bonding donor acidity efficiently enhance the detection sensitivity and realize reusable turn-on detection based on the adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism. Consequently, CDs@MIL-101 exhibits high sensitivity (detection limit: 0.036% and 0.028% v/v), wide response range (0.04–100% and 0.03–45%) and reusable stability for liquid water detection in acetone and tetrahydrofuran, respectively. Meanwhile, it can also detect gaseous water and the detection range is 23% to 85%. The analysis of real samples confirms the proposed method. Such construction strategy and detection mechanism will offer guidance for optimizing and expanding the detection performance of CDs, which is of great significance in the design and construction of novel multiphase sensors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication a reusable and highly sensitive fluorescence sensor in a controllable manner for water detection in solvents and humidity remains a top priority target yet a challenging task. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) are successfully implanted into confined metal–organic framework MIL-101(Cr) cages via “ship in the bottle” strategy and the confined CDs are firstly observed in MIL-101(Cr) cage showing high dispersion and high N content by advanced iDPC STEM technique. The highly selective water capture and sensitive changes of the fluorescence property with solution polarity and hydrogen-bonding donor acidity efficiently enhance the detection sensitivity and realize reusable turn-on detection based on the adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism. Consequently, CDs@MIL-101 exhibits high sensitivity (detection limit: 0.036% and 0.028% v/v), wide response range (0.04–100% and 0.03–45%) and reusable stability for liquid water detection in acetone and tetrahydrofuran, respectively. Meanwhile, it can also detect gaseous water and the detection range is 23% to 85%. The analysis of real samples confirms the proposed method. Such construction strategy and detection mechanism will offer guidance for optimizing and expanding the detection performance of CDs, which is of great significance in the design and construction of novel multiphase sensors.

A reusable turn-on and visual fluorescence sensor of CDs@MIL-101 was initially constructed via "ship in the bottle" strategy for liquid and gaseous water detection. Based on adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism, CDs@MIL-101 exhibited high sensitivity, wide response range and reusable stability. It offers guidance for optimizing and expanding detection performance of CDs to design and construct novel multiphase fluorescence sensors.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is generally considered as the most common pollutant and impurity in organic solvents and dry products. Numerous classic organic reactions need to occur under strict anhydrous conditions and the trace amount of water in the reaction mediums of these reactions not only influences the activity and product quality, but also may pose safety issues, particularly in the fields of pharmaceutical manufacturing [1], chemosynthesis and food inspection [2]. Therefore, it is essential to detect trace water rapidly and quantitatively, water detection is attracting great interest nowadays.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been proposed to be used as luminescent sensors for water detection owing to their unique fluorescent properties derived from their rich surface groups and quantum effects [3]. Because of their outstanding merits in terms of their simple operation, rapid, sensitive and real-time detection, CDs are receiving growing attention. For instance, Anfeng Chen used resorcinol to synthesize the CDs as a fluorescence quenching sensor for water detection in organic solvents. Based on non-radiative electron transfer between CDs and water molecules, it exhibited real-time sensitive detection with a limit of detection of 0.006% (v/v) [4]. Jongsung Kim reported the novel N-doped CDs using β-resorcylic acid and ethylenediamine to detect water content in the acetone based on quenching fluorometry, demonstrating a detection limit as low as 0.37% (v/v) within 5 min [5]. Qingbiao Zhao prepared biomass-based carbon dots (BD-CDs) by using Cinnamomum plants to detect water and achieved a detection limit of 0.022% (v/v) [6]. Nevertheless, bare CDs are extremely prone to aggregate into large sizes, resulting in poor fluorescence stability and recyclability [7,8]. Furthermore, low adsorption and nonspecific quenching of CDs would negatively affect their sensitivity and anti-interference properties [9,10]. Consequently, developing fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity, high reusability as well as high interference is still a great challenge for the detection of trace water in organic solvents.

In order to improve the detection sensitivity and fluorescence stability, several research teams have immobilized carbon dots on solid supports such as inorganic salts, polymer matrices and porous materials to fabricate carbon dot-based nanocomposites [11]. Among them, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are appealing nanoporous materials constructed by assembling organic linkers and metal ions. They possess ultra-high specific surface area, controllable pore structure and high density of metal sites, which endows them with unique adsorption and selectivity towards many small molecules [12]. Especially, the existence of unsaturated metal sites in MOFs is responsible for their high affinity towards H2O molecules [13], which makes MOF-based fluorescence sensors be widely applied as supporters in the application of water detection [14,15]. For instance, Bing Yan's research group used In-MOF to wrap CDs by post-growth of In-MOFs to construct a ratio fluorescence sensor, which achieved good anti-interference ability and realized the sensitive detection of liquid - gas water [16]. Yuwu Chi and co-workers doped CDs into Eu-MOF via the adsorption strategy to construct a water fluorescence sensor, which exhibited the limit of detection as low as 0.03% (v/v) [17]. The above reported CD@MOFs composites have promoted detector stability and sensitivity to a certain extent. However, top-down CDs loading approach for CDs immobilization in MOFs suffered from the following limitations, including CDs particle agglomeration, poor adhesion to MOFs and nonuniform CDs encapsulated in MOFs [18,19]. These issues would affect the detection sensitivity and reusability performance of the probes.

In this work, “ship in the bottle” strategy was proposed to confined growth of CDs in MIL-101(Cr) cages to construct a high-performance CDs@MOFs fluorescence sensor. CDs precursors was introduced into MIL-101(Cr) cages with high dispersion by using double solvent method (DSM) and then in-situ grown into CDs under the confined space of MIL-101(Cr) cages. As a novel and unique adsorption platform, MIL-101(Cr) can efficiently capture water from organic solvents, which can further increase the contact opportunity between confined CDs and water molecules in the MIL-101(Cr) pores. At the same time, the loading of CDs will lead to the emergence of new micropores, which will strengthen the capillary of water molecules at low concentrations. Thus, it could significantly promote the adsorption towards water molecules. Moreover, the highly dispersed and nitrogen-rich confined CDs inside of MOFs were more sensitive to the fluorescence response of water molecules. Different from other sensors, CDs in our designed CDs@MIL-101 possessed bi-function of adsorption site and signal generation site for water capture and detection, which can intensify solvent effect (polarity and hydrogen donation) and enhance fluorescence signal. Thus, CDs@MIL-101 formed turn-on and visual fluorescence sensor for liquid-gas water detection with high sensitivity and reusable stability. Herein, integrated differential phase contrast (iDPC) STEM imaging technique was used to investigate the existence status of these confirmed CDs in MIL-101(Cr) cages. The unique properties induced by the confinement effect, such as surface group interactions and optical features, were characterized for CDs@MIL-101. Fluorescence and visual detections of water in different solvents and humid air were carried out and the detection mechanism was systematically investigated. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on dual-phase water detection using CDs based on a turn-on fluorescence, which might open up a new promising route in enhancing trace water detection and offer guidance for optimizing and expanding the detection performance of CDs.

Terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%), chromic (III) nitrate, monohydrate citric acid, urea, nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O, 99%) and tetrahydrofuran (THF, ≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Aladdin reagents (Shanghai, China). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, > 99.8%), dioxane, ethanol (CH3CH2OH, > 99.7%), 1-propanol, 2-propanol, butanol, ethyl acetate, n-butanol (n-BuOH, ≥ 99.5%), acetonitrile, methanol (MeOH, 99.5%), acetone, 1,4-dioxane and hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40%) were obtained from Lan Tian Chemical Co, Ltd (Nanning, China). The experimental water was deionized water and all the materials were analytical grade with no further purification.

Synthesis of MIL-101 (Cr): The preparation of MIL-101(Cr) was carried out using the conventional solvothermal synthesis method reported previously with slight modification [20]. First of all, Cr (NO3)3·9 H2O, H2BDC, HF and H2O were fully dissolved and mixed in the molar composition ratio of 1:1:1:278. Secondly, the mixed solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 493 K for 8 h and naturally cooled to room temperature. The green color MIL-101(Cr) was formed but a large amount of recrystallized H2BDC was also produced during the reaction. In order to remove impurities and obtain crystal materials with specific surface area and high porosity, they were washed three times each with hot ethanol and DMF. Finally, the product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The MIL-101(Cr) crystal material was obtained by vacuum drying at 423 K for 12 h to remove residual solvents.

Synthesis of CDs@MIL-101 : It was well recognized that the hydrophilic inner surface area of MIL-101(Cr) was far higher than the hydrophobic outer surface area [21]. A small amount of aqueous citric acid and urea could enter into the hydrophilic pores by capillary force, which significantly minimizing citric acid and urea deposition on the outer surface. For this reason, CDs@MIL-101 was prepared by using a double-solvents method for carbon dots loading ( Scheme 1) [21,22]. First, activated MIL-101(Cr) (100 mg) was added to n-hexane (20 mL) and sonicated for 15 min until it became homogeneous. Secondly, the mixture was transferred to a magnetic stirrer for 2 h of continuous stirring, and 0.1 mL of citric acid and urea mixture with different molar ratios of 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8 and 1:10 were slowly dropped with continuous stirring, respectively. After that, the mixtures were continuously stirred at room temperature for 12 h, filtered, then dried in an N2 atmosphere at 80 °C for 12 h. The products were then calcined at 180 °C under N2 atmosphere for 8 h to transform citric acid and urea into carbon dots in MIL-101(Cr) cages. Following that, the products were washed with ethanol three times and centrifuged to remove remaining citric acid and urea. Finally, they were vacuum-dried at 80 °C to obtain the final products CDsxx@MIL-101, which was designated as CDs1:2 @MIL-101, CDs1:4 @MIL-101, CDs@MIL-101, CDs1:8 @MIL-101 and CDs1:10 @MIL-101, respectively.

Synthesis of bare carbon dots (CDs) : Citric acid and urea were used as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively, and water was used as a solvent for the reaction medium in the preparation of carbon dots. Firstly, monohydrate citric acid (2.1 g) and urea were (3.6 g) mixed and completely dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL). Next, the reaction mixture was dried at 80 °C for 12 h and calcined at 180 °C in N2 for 8 h to transform citric acid and urea into carbon dots. Afterwards, the product was dispersed in an ethanol solvent and centrifuged to discard large particles. The residual citric acid and urea were removed using a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 72 h. Finally, freeze-drying was employed to obtain unconfined CDs powder, which was dispersed in organic solvent (acetone or THF) for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water detection using CDs@MIL-101 and bare CDs as probes
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, different volumes of water were added to a clean quartz colorimetric tube using a precision micropipette, followed by the addition of tetrahydrofuran to obtain a concentration of water ranging from 0% to 45% (v/v). Dry CDs@MIL-101 was added to keep its concentration at 0.5 mg mL−1 and after 1 min of sonication, the luminescence spectra of the solution was recorded through a glass filter (>300 nm) under an excitation wavelength of 240 nm. The detection of water content in acetone was performed in a similar manner, except that the excitation wavelength was changed to 320 nm. For comparison, the prepared unconfined CDs was diluted into tetrahydrofuran or acetone solutions, and its photoluminescence spectra for different water contents in organic solvents were recorded under the same detection conditions as CDs@MIL-101, as reported in ref [23]. In this work, the data close to the Rayleigh scattering line were set as 0 to eliminate the interfere of the Rayleigh scattering on the result analysis in three-dimensional (3-D) fluorescence mappings [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative humidity detection using CDs@MIL-101 as a probe
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the water vapor adsorption performance of CDs@MIL-101 at room temperature (25 °C) and atmospheric pressure (101.3 KPa), the following experimental setup was constructed. As shown in Scheme 2, the relative humidity of the experimental apparatus decreased monotonically as the flow rate of dry nitrogen in path 2 increased. Since the bubbler was filled with deionized water, the relative humidity increased as the flow rate of air path 1 increased. The humidity sensor can effectively monitor the relative humidity of the examination room and the change of sample quality in the examination room was recorded by the electronic balance. The surge chamber can intercept large-particle droplets and the vent holes can effectively maintain the balance of air pressure inside and outside the examination room, thereby avoiding the influence of pressure changes on the weighing result.

The details were described as follows: firstly, the relative humidity of the examination room was kept stable by adjusting the gas flow of paths 1 and 2. Then, dried CDs@MIL-101 was placed in the examination room and the change in sample weight over time was recorded. When the sample was saturated with adsorption, disperse it into the tetrahydrofuran solution and the luminescence spectra of the solution were recorded after 1 min of sonication using a glass cut-off filter (>300 nm) under an excitation wavelength of 240 nm. The fluorescence intensity corresponding to different humidity was obtained by changing the gas flow and repeating the above steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energy of detected solvent molecules and affinity simulation on MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of MIL-101(Cr) for acetone, water and tetrahydrofuran molecule was investigated by using Adsorption Locator module in Materials Studio 8.0. The three-dimensional structures of solvents molecules were derived from Pubchem database [25] and the crystal structure file of MIL-101 (Cr) was downloaded from the Open Crystal Database [26], COD ID:4000663. The geometric structure of CDs@MIL-101 was optimized by using Density functional theory (DFT) and all the results were within the category of generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with PW91 functional. Nuclear electrons and atomic orbitals were deal with by using the half-nuclear pseudopotential (DSPP) approximation and the d-polarized basis set (DND) approximation respectively. The energy change, gradient and displacemen for convergence criterion of the geometrical optimization was 2.0 × 10−5 hartree, 4.0 × 10−3 hartree per Å and 5.0 × 10−3 Å, respectively [27,28].

CDsxx@MIL-101 was prepared using citric acid and urea as CDs precursor by double-solvents method and the fluorescence intensity of these probes were shown in Fig. S1. As seem, the fluorescence intensity of the probes varied with the molar ratio of the reactants and reached the strongest when the molar ratio was 1:6. This may be caused by the change of nitrogen and oxygen-rich groups on the surface of the materials [29]. For stable and distinct fluorescence emission, the probe should have a strong fluorescence. Therefore, CDs@MIL-101 prepared from the molar ratio of 1:6 was selected as the detector for the detection of water content. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101 were presented in Fig. S2a-b. As shown in Fig. S2a, MIL-101(Cr) displayed octahedral nanocrystals with uniform size within the range of 500–600 nm and a Zeta potential of 25.2 mV [30], confirming the perfect crystallinity of synthesized MIL-101(Cr). After the introduction of CDs in MIL-101(Cr), no apparent difference in morphology or small particles agglomeration was observed on the surfaces of MIL-101(Cr) (Fig. S2b). However, its Zeta potential significantly decreased to 7.1 mV. This decrease was caused by the negative effect of bare CDs’ Zeta potential (−28.6 mV) on MOFs (Fig. S3), which indicated the successful participation of CDs in MIL-101(Cr).

To gain insight into the distribution and growth status of the confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr) cages, we employed the iDPC technique in a transmission electron microscope to directly image the crystal structure of CDs@MIL-101 samples with high resolution. The iDPC imaging with extreme low dose of electrons impinging onto sample has recently been proven to be an efficient high-resolution imaging method for beam-sensitive materials [31]. Fig. 1a schematically illustrated two types of giant cages (outlined in red and blue circles) in the MIL-101(Cr) framework and Fig. 1b showed these featured frameworks overlaid onto the iDPC images. Fig. 1c-e showed the detailed structures inside the MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101, which revealed that no large particles of CDs were observed in CDs@MIL-101. This implied that these confined CDs with particle size less than 2 nm, might be highly dispersed within MIL-101(Cr). In addition, iDPC-STEM images became clearer with an increasing amount of CDs in MIL-101(Cr), which might be attributed to the enhanced electrical conductivity of CDs@MIL-101 materials with more CDs filled. This was also consisted with the EIS measured results in Fig. S4. Notably, more defects appeared in the structure of 0.2 mL-CDs@MIL-101 and its corresponding fluorescence intensity was increased as the amount of CDs in MIL-101(Cr) increases. It was indicated that CDs loading can be precisely regulated to adjust fluorescence intensity via double-solvents method and the carbon dots were highly dispersed in the MIL-101 framework with the particles less than 2 nm.

Main diffraction peaks of MIL-101(Cr) with high intensity (seen in Fig. 2a) appeared at 2θ = 5.32°, 5.91°, 8.48°, 9.10°, 10.36° and 16.57°, demonstrating the successful preparation of pure MIL-101(Cr) with high crystallinity [32]. The XRD profile of bare CDs showed diffraction peaks at 25.03° and 27.12°, representing the characteristic interplanar stacking of aromatic systems, which was indexed to the (002) peak of graphitic nitrogen. The diffraction peak at 12.03° corresponded to an interplanar structural packing motif indexed to the (100) plane [33]. As expected, the XRD pattern profile of CDs@MIL-101 was similar with pristine MIL-101(Cr), suggesting that immobilized CDs had no detrimental impact on the MIL-101(Cr) structure. Interestingly, no typical diffraction peaks of CDs were found in the CDs@MIL-101 composite, indicating that no CDs aggregation occurred as a result of confined-grown CDs in MIL-101(Cr). The XRD findings of CDs@MIL-101 demonstrated that the CDs were embedded in the pores of MIL-101(Cr) with high dispersion rather than a physical mixture of MIL-101(Cr) and CDs in the CDs@MIL-101 composite, which combined with its corresponding SEM and TEM results.

Fig. 2b displays the TGA and DTG curves for evaluating the thermal stability of the CDs@MIL-101 and the probable interaction between the confined CDs and MIL-101(Cr) pore cages. For MIL-101(Cr), the weight loss can be separated into three stages, which were assigned to the removal of adsorbed water (300 ∼ 450 K), loss of OH/F group (450 ∼ 650 K), and structure decomposition (650 ∼ 800 K), respectively [34]. For the bare CDs using unconfined synthesis, two stages of weight loss were noticed at peak temperatures of 443.2 and 562.3 K, which represent adsorbed water and its structure collapse, respectively. After the immobilization of CDs in MOF, the TGA and DTG curves of CDs@MIL-101 displayed a nearly similar weight-loss trend to that of parent MIL-101(Cr), with the exception of the emergence of low-intensity CDs peak at 556.3 K. Moreover, CDs@MIL-101 showed an increase in weight loss from 70.0% to 73.9%, which inferred the existence of CDs in MIL-101(Cr). Similarly, a slightly lower peak temperature for CDs within CDs@MIL-101 demonstrated that these confined CDs had smaller sizes and altered surface properties than unconfined CDs. Concomitantly, CDs@MIL-101 displayed a clearly higher decomposition temperature (740.1 K) than MIL-101(Cr) (719.6 K), which may be primarily attributed to the coordination of amide bonds or hydroxyl groups on CDs surfaces with metal sites in MIL-101(Cr) [21]. Hence, immobilization of CDs in MOFs was proved to enhance the thermal stability of MIL-101(Cr).

Fig. 2c shows the results of the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution of MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101 using the NLDFT model, which were carried out to investigate the confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr) pore cages. By comparison, the adsorption isotherms of both MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101 were similar to type I curves apart from their difference in N2 adsorption uptakes. The obtained pore structure parameters shown in Table S1, it is evident that the corresponding BET, pore volume and average pore diameters of MIL-101(Cr) were apparently decreased after the CDs encapsulation within MIL-101(Cr) pores. The parent MIL-101(Cr) showed a high BET surface area of 2821 m2/g and a pore volume of 1.53 cm3/g. For the CDs@MIL-101, these values were decreased to 35% of those for MIL-101(Cr), which were 975 m2/g and 0.54 cm3/g, respectively. Interestingly, the pore size distribution curve of CDs@MIL-101 exhibited a negative shift of concentrated peaks with lower intensity (Fig. 2c inset). Moreover, a new peak of micropores at around 5.8 Å appeared. This confirmed the confined growth of CDs in pore cages of MIL-101(Cr) with high dispersion, which resulted in pore size reduction of MIL-101(Cr). Furthermore, the newly generated micropores indicated the remaining space in the MOF cages after CDs immobilization, which can further facilitate water adsorption [13,32].

The functional group composition of MIL-101(Cr), bare CDs, and CDs@MIL-101 was analyzed by FT-IR, with spectra scanned in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 (Fig. 2d). For pristine MIL-101(Cr), the absorption peak near 588 cm−1 belonged to the characteristic peak of Cr-O stretching vibration. The peaks at 747 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1 were derived from the out-of-plane bending vibration and deformation of C-H on the ligand benzene ring, while the peaks at 1628 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations and O-CO symmetric stretch of organic ligand dicarboxylates, respectively [34,35]. Similarly, the broad peak at 3441 cm−1 originated from the water molecules or hydroxyl groups attached to the surface of MIL-101(Cr). The above results preliminarily indicate the successful synthesis of MIL-101(Cr). For bare CDs, the peaks at 1719 cm−1, 1615 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1 were assigned to amide bonds I, II and III, respectively. It is interesting that CDs@MIL-101 exhibited both the characteristic absorption peaks of MIL-101(Cr) and CDs, evidencing that the CDs were successfully loaded into the MIL-101(Cr) skeleton. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs@MIL-101, a corresponding absorption peak of CDs near 1719 cm−1 was observed, which was assigned to the characteristic CO high-frequency vibration peak of amide bonds I [36]. It is remarkable to see that the intensity of this peak in CDs@MIL-101 was obviously higher than that in bare CDs. Apparently, the confined CDs exhibited different surface properties compared with unconfined CDs and contained a much higher number of amide bonds. The higher amount of amide groups in confined CDs may be caused by two reasons. Firstly, the molecular size of urea is smaller than citric acid, which preferentially entered MIL-101(Cr) pores and participated in the reaction with citric acid under confined conditions. Secondly, the confined CDs had smaller particle sizes compared to unconfined CDs, which facilitated -COOH decomposition under low-temperature calcination [33]. As a consequence, a higher content of amide groups remained in confined CDs. Besides, other characteristic peaks of amide bonds were also observed in CDs@MIL-101. The peaks at 1618 cm−1 and 1195 cm−1 were attributed to amide bond II and amide bond III, respectively, which were overlapped with the absorption peak of MIL-101(Cr). The presence of N-H groups in composite has also been confirmed by observing a peak at 3185 cm−1. The above information proved that confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr) contains more abundant functional groups.

To further confirm the elemental composition and bond configuration of bare CDs, original MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101, XPS measurements were conducted and the results were presented in Figs. S5, S6 and 2e-f. Fig. S5 depicted the XPS survey spectra of unconfined CDs, MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101. It could be seen that the percentages of C and O in unconfined CDs were 40.0 at% and 30.5 at%, respectively, and were 59.9 at% and 35.8 at% in MIL-101(Cr). Notably, both C and O percentages in CDs@MIL-101 were not within the scope of unconfined CDs nor MIL-101(Cr). The C content in CDs@MIL-101 was 61.3 at%, which was higher than its parent materials, while the O in CDs@MIL-101 content was 29.5 at%, which was lower than the parent materials. It clearly implied that the confined growth of CDs in CDs@MIL-101 differed from unconfined CDs in terms of structural evolution. Compared to unconfined CDs, confined CDs in CDs@MIL-101 possessed a higher C content but a lower O content. The main possible cause of this phenomenon was the mentioned spatial confinement of MIL-101(Cr) cages. As discussed in the above characterization, more amide groups were found in confined CDs, inferring that a higher content of urea was involved in the bottom-up growth of CDs compared to unconfined CDs. Moreover, more -COOH would have been decomposed from these smaller-sized confined CDs. As expected, these confined CDs showed much higher percentages of N and C but a lower percentage of O [33,37].

As demonstrated in Fig. 2e, the O 1 s spectrum of unconfined CDs was divided into two peaks, corresponding to C-O and CO at 533.1 and 531.8 eV. The MIL-101(Cr) possessed three typical peaks at 533.1, 532.0 and 530.6 eV, belonging to C-O, CO, and Cr-O, respectively [32,38]. Compared to MIL-101(Cr), the Cr-O peak in CDs@MIL-101 was slightly shifted to lower binding energy (BE =530.4 eV) and its percentage content increased slightly. This phenomenon could be explained by the fact that hydroxyl groups on the confined CDs collaborated to coordinate with Cr sites of MIL-101(Cr) [32]. Simultaneously, the position of the CO peak in CDs@MIL-101 shifted to lower BE (531.8 eV), with a significant increase in content from 63.9% to 71.1%. Meanwhile, the C-O bond content decreased significantly from 26.5% to 18.4%. It can be speculated that the confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr) had a much higher amount of amide bonds than the unconfined CDs, indicating the structural evolution of confined CDs growth in CDs@MIL-101 was different from these unconfined CDs. The Cr 2p spectra of MIL-101(Cr) and CDs@MIL-101 were also provided in Fig. S6. For the MIL-101(Cr), the peaks located at 577.5 and 587.0 eV suggested the existence of Cr-O, which was in accordance with literature [39]. In the Cr 2p spectrum of CDs@MIL-101, these two peaks were slightly shifted to lower binding energy (BE = 577.3 eV and 586.8 eV), implying an increase in electron cloud density of Cr3+ metal sites after the introduction of CDs to MIL-101(Cr). Along with this, the emergence of the peak at 575.6 eV indicated the presence of Cr-N, which was ascribed to the successful grafting of the amide nitrogen on the surface of CDs to Cr3+ and confirmed the interaction of confined CDs with MIL-101(Cr) [40].

Furthermore, the N 1 s spectra of bare CDs and CDs@MIL-101 were explored and the results were summarized in Fig. 2f. The N 1 s spectrum of bare CDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks, including pyridinic-N (399.7 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.5 eV) and N-H (401.2 eV), respectively [41,42]. After introducing CDs into MIL-101(Cr), a new peak at 398.9 eV corresponding to Cr-N appeared, which was consistent with the findings of the Cr 2p spectrum of CDs@MIL-101 (Fig. S6). Besides, the C-N peaks in CDs@MIL-101 were slightly shifted to higher binding energy (BE=399.9 eV). It indicated that amide groups of CDs were successfully grafted on MIL-101(Cr) via Cr-N coordination and thereby N donated some electrons to Cr clusters [40,43]. Interestingly, compared to bare CDs, there was a significant decrease in the amount of CN and N-H peaks, but an increase in the C-N amount in CDs@MIL-101. It directly proved that CDs fabricated via confined growth in MIL-101(Cr) contained more abundant N-containing functional groups such as amide bonds [44] than unconfined CDs. This result was well consistent with the findings of the above-mentioned characterization in Fig. 2e and FT-IR in Fig. 2d.

To search the proper excitation wavelength for sensitive water detection, three-dimensional (3-D) fluorescence mappings of CDs@MIL-101, parent MIL-101(Cr) and unconfined CDs were measured in water and the results were shown in Figs. 3a and S7. By contrast, the parent MIL-101(Cr) showed almost no FL response in the excitation wavelength with a range of 200–600 nm (Fig. S7a). The unconfined CDs had a strong FL emission in the range of 450–570 nm at the excitation (Ex) wavelength of 230–450 nm (Fig. S7b). Unlike unconfined CDs and pure MIL-101(Cr), CDs@MIL-101 had three FL emission (Em) centers in pure water, which were labeled as center-1 (Ex=270 nm, Em=510 nm), center-2 (Ex=330 nm, Em=420 nm) and center-3 (Ex=400 nm, Em=505 nm) in Fig. 3a. The changed FL emission of CDs@MIL-101 verified the evolution of optical property in confined CDs due to the quantum effect of particle size change and interfacial interaction between CDs and MOFs [45,46]. In addition, the 3D emission profiles of CDs@MIL-101 were recorded in a series of common organic solvents with different polarities, as shown in Figs. 3b,c and S8. As observed, CDs@MIL-101 displayed single emission centers in pure acetone (Ex=410 nm, Em=485 nm) and tetrahydrofuran (Ex=420 nm, Em=480 nm). Due to the optical difference between water and acetone/tetrahydrofuran, water content in acetone and tetrahydrofuran can be detected based on turn-on FL by selecting a reasonable excitation wavelength [23]. In order to intuitively show the discrepancy districts, their choice of excitation wavelength was overlapped and presented in Figs. 3d and S8. As one can see, based on the chosen criterion that the fluorescence intensity (>3500) of the probe in water is more than three times that in organic solvents (>1167), the possible chosen area should be in the corresponding excitation wavelength intersection of water-responsive emission center and the solvent non/or weak-responsive emission area. Therefore, FL would be turned on by water without being covered by an organic solvent. For example, center-1 area with Ex from 223 to 287 nm wavelength (Fig. 3d) can be chosen as the turn-on fluorescence for water detection in tetrahydrofuran solvent, which was not overlapped by the FL response area of tetrahydrofuran (the black left slash area). When the excitation wavelength was set to about 240 nm, the FL intensity was displayed to be nearly 32 times higher in water than in pure tetrahydrofuran solution and the FL emission peak position was shown to be red-shifted from 478 to 512 nm (Fig. S9a). Similarly, when the excitation wavelength was chosen at 320 nm in the center-2 areas for water detection in acetone, the fluorescence intensity was about 70 times higher in water than in acetone solution with a red-shift from 400 to 412 nm (Fig. S9b). Thus, it displayed the turn-on and color-visual fluorescence property for water detection in solvents instead of fluorescence quenching, which may demonstrate the merits of high detection sensitivity and anti-interference ability [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-on FL and visual detection of liquid-gas water using CDs@MIL− 101
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a presents photoluminescence (PL) intensity of CDs@MIL-101 in response to water content (0.03–45%) in tetrahydrofuran under 240 nm excitation. As shown, the PL intensity of CDs@MIL-101 showed an increasing trend with the increase of water content in tetrahydrofuran, demonstrating a good linear relationship between the PL intensity and water content (Fig. 4b). In addition, its PL emission peak had a significant red-shift from 482 to 508 nm and thus exhibited a visual property of color and FL intensity changes (top of Fig. 4b). Moreover, CDs@MIL-101 can also detect water content in the acetone solvent. As the water content in acetone increased from 0% to 100%, the PL intensity of CDs@MIL-101 in the mixed solution gradually increased with a good linear relationship and the emission showed a red-shift of the peak from 403 nm to 412 nm under a single excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 4c).

Based on a ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, the detection limits of water in tetrahydrofuran and acetone were 0.028% and 0.036%, respectively. The proposed CDs@MIL-101 sensor has a wide linear range, a low detection limit and a good cycle stability for water detection, which is obviously superior to other similar sensors ( Table 1 and Table 2). What’s more interesting was that the fluorescent color of the solution gradually turned from blue to green as the water content increased. As illustrated in Fig. 4b and d, when the water content in tetrahydrofuran and acetone increased from 0.2% to 4%, then to 12% and from 0.8% to 10%, then to 35%, respectively, the FL color of the solution changed from blue to cyan and finally to green. These color changes can be easily observable with the naked eye under the excitation of 365 nm (using a portable UV lamp) [5,47].

In order to illustrate the superiority of CDs@MIL-101 in water detection, bare carbon dots (unconfined CDs) were used as a probe to detect water content in organic solvents for comparison (Fig. S10). The detection limits of unconfined CDs in tetrahydrofuran and acetone were 1.38% and 0.66% (v/v), respectively, and the limit of detection (LOD) were one order of magnitude higher than that of CDs@MIL-101. Contemporaneously, the detection ranges of unconfined CDs were reduced from 0.04% to 100% to 0.8–70% in acetone and from 0.03% to 45% to 1.5–45% in THF. Meanwhile, in order to test the reusability of CDs@MIL-101, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIL-101 responding to 0% and 10% (v/v) water in acetone were recorded repeatedly. As depicted in Fig. S11a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIL-101 remained highly stable after five times cycling runs. It demonstrated good immobilization of confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr) cages, which preventing them from escaping the MIL-101(Cr) cages and aids them for repetitive detection of water. Furthermore, the influence of different solvents on the stability of the CDs@MIL-101 framework was also explored and the results were provided in Fig. S11b. As shown, the frame structure of CDs@MIL-101 remained basically unchanged after five detection times, implying that CDs@MIL-101 can be recycled after repeated use. To verify the accuracy of the proposed method, the gas chromatography (GC) method with higher accuracy was employ for the detection of tetrahydrofuran and acetone content in real samples using a standard calibration curve [23]. The relative errors of the gas chromatography method and fluorescence analysis in tetrahydrofuran and acetone were 1.4% and 0.7%, respectively (Table S2). Therefore, this fluorescence analysis method exhibited a high level of accuracy and practicability.

MIL-101(Cr) has been well reported to have the adsorption merit for capturing water molecules [13]. Based on this capability, CDs@MIL-101 was proposed to detect gaseous water in the humid air. To achieve this, a water vapor detection device (shown in Scheme 2) was set up for the purpose of simulating different humidity environments to conduct water vapor adsorption experiments under normal temperature (298.15 K) and pressure (101.3 KPa). Fig. S12 depicts the relationship between the mass percentage of CDs@MIL-101 and relative humidity at normal temperature and normal pressure. It can be seen that with an increase of adsorption time, the adsorption capacity of water vapor gradually increased until it reached equilibrium. Moreover, the equilibrium time was gradually shortened with the increase of humidity. In order to ensure the adsorbed equilibrium was reached, the CDs@MIL-101 sensor was exposed to humid air for about 2 h and then immersed in tetrahydrofuran to prepare a solution of fixed concentration. The air relative humidity was indirectly detected by the change in fluorescence intensity of the solution in the relative humidity range of 23–85% (Fig. 4e). The fitting results have a good linear relationship (Fig. 4f), indicating that CDs@MIL-101 has the potential to detect relative humidity of the air. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of utilizing carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for liquid-gas phase water detection based on an on-turn fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism for enhancement of turn-on fluorescence on CDs@MIL-101 sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Besides of the container for CDs, MIL-101(Cr) with high surface area and adsorption ability towards H2O molecule can significantly improve the water detection sensitivity. The adsorption affinity and selective adsorption behavior of CDs@MIL-101(Cr) for H2O would influence the sensitivity and resolution for water detection [13,18]. Therefore, theoretical simulations were carried out to calculate the adsorption position and adsorption energy of water, tetrahydrofuran and acetone in relation to Cr clusters of MIL-101(Cr) at the molecular level using density functional theory (DFT) and Material Studio 8, the results were shown in Figs. 5a-c and S13. As depicted, the adsorption sites of water in MIL-101(Cr) was approached the Cr sites cluster, which was due to its hard Lewis acid sites attraction towards the hard base of H2O molecules, with an adsorption energy of − 74.7 kJ/mol. This energy value was attributed to the strong adsorption of water molecules on MIL-101(Cr), which would facilitate the sensor to selectively capture water from other solvents and accelerate water diffusion to the confined CDs in MIL-101(Cr). By comparison, the adsorption energies of acetone and tetrahydrofuran molecules in MIL-101(Cr) were − 60.4 and − 66.3 kJ/mol, respectively, which were significantly lower than that of water. Moreover, the volume of water molecules (117.44 Å3) is much smaller than that of acetone (274.05 Å3) and tetrahydrofuran (309.89 Å3) molecules as demonstrated in Fig. S13, which implied that the steric hindrance of water molecules was lower than that of tetrahydrofuran and acetone molecules during the adsorption process, resulting in faster diffusion of water in MIL-101(Cr) pores than tetrahydrofuran and acetone. Combining the effects of binding energy and steric hindrance, water molecules exhibited diffusion and competitive adsorption merits over tetrahydrofuran and acetone, which can further increase the contact opportunity between the CDs and water molecules in the MIL-101(Cr) pores, and thus improving the detection sensitivity of the CDs@MIL-101.

For comparison, pure urea, pure citric acid, and urea + citric acid were implanted into MIL-101(Cr) to synthesize three composites, which were named as CDs-U@MIL-101, CDs-CA@MIL-101 and CDs@MIL-101. As seen in Fig. 5d, CDs-U@MIL-101 and CDs-CA@MIL-101 exhibited an absorption peak at 285 and 240 nm in aqueous solution, respectively. They belonged to the absorption peaks resulting from π-π * electron transitions of sp2 hybrid aromatic carbons. For CDs@MIL-101, it almost maintained the UV absorption at 240 and 285 nm (π-π * electron transitions) and displayed a new absorption peak at 411 nm corresponding to the typical n-π * transition of abundant amido bonds in confined CDs [15,48]. This could be ascribed to the fact that the lone pair of electrons of the nitrogen atom in the amide bonds could be transferred more easily to the lowest unoccupied energy level of the inner carbon nucleus, resulting in a reduction of the bandgap energy level and a red-shift of the absorption peak. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5e,f, CDs-U@MIL-101 exhibited almost no fluorescence response in aqueous solution, whereas CDs-CA@MIL-101 had much lower fluorescence intensity in aqueous solution than CDs@MIL-101. Therefore, the formation of amide bonds from the reaction of urea and citric acid played an indispensable role in the construction of highly sensitive fluorescence probes for H2O detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent effect on enhanced turn-on fluorescence on CDs@MIL-101
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the solvent effect on the turn-on fluorescence performance of CDs@MIL-101, 3-D FL mappings of CDs@MIL-101 in a series of conventional organic solvents were measured and were presented in Fig. S8. It can be seen that the regions of FL response emission for water with emission intensity (water region) higher than 3500 and for solvent with emission intensity (solvent region) higher than 1167 was chosen for overlapping region calculation. The overlap percentage was calculated as the ratio of solvent region to total water region, which was then depicted in Fig. 6a. A higher overlapping percentage means that a narrower range of FL extraction wavelength can be chosen for water detection by using CDs@MIL-101 via the turn-on FL approach. Based on their overlap rate, Fig. 6a was approximately divided into three detection regions, including a high sensitivity region (< 65%, light green region), a transition sensitivity region (65–80%, light yellow region) and an unsuitable detection region (>80%, light red region). According to this method, it can be judged the detection possibility and approximate sensitivity by only measuring their 3-D FL mappings.

To investigate the detection mechanism of the CDs@MIL-101 sensor, we further plotted the overlap rate as the function of relative polarity (E
T
N ) in Fig. 6b. Apparently, there was no obvious linear relationship between the overlap rate and solvent polarity (E
T
N ). Probably, some additional interactions between the solvents and CDs@MIL-101 sensor may also influence the sensor’s FL response performance. In the case, the hydrogen-bonding donor acidity (α) of these solvents was also considered in this work and their values were listed in Table S3. Fig. 6c showed the relationship between the overlap rate and E
T
N of these selected solvents having no hydrogen-bonding donor acidity (α = 0), which displayed an approximately linear relationship (R2 = 0.97). This indicated that the overlap rate is proportional to solvent polarity E
T
N under similar solvent α values. Moreover, Fig. 6d showed the relationship between the overlap rate and α (α ≠0) of these selected solvents having different E
T
N , which was also found a linear dependence (R2 = 0.96). These results suggested that the hydrogen-bonding donor acidity (α) of the solvent played a dominant role in affecting the overlap ratio between solvents and water. The smaller values of α, the lower overlap ratio between the solvents and water, which makes it higher feasibility for water detection in this solvent. Based on this, detection conditions (i.e.,excitation wavelength) and the feasibility for water in unknown solvents can be rapidly predicted based on the relevancy of their known polarity and hydrogen-bonding donor acidity. However, since the selective sensitivity of CDs@MIL-101 for water depends on the difference between the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of water and the solvent. Detection sensitivity would be disturbed by their similarity of fluorescence characteristic between water and solvent being tested, which would limit its application to a certain extent.

In summary, CDs@MIL-101 was designed via “ship in the bottle” strategy for the first time to function as reusable turn-on fluorescence and visual sensor for water detection in solvents and humidity. The advanced iDPC STEM technique was successfully observed the lattice of MIL-101(Cr) and the dispersion status of CDs in CDs@MIL-101, which confirmed that these confined CDs were highly dispersed within MIL-101(Cr) cages in the form of smaller than 2 nm. CDs@MIL-101 was investigated to have high sensitivity and color resolution for liquid-gas water detection. For liquid water detection, LOD of CDs@MIL-101 for water content in acetone and tetrahydrofuran can reach as low as 0.036% (v/v) and 0.028%, respectively, and the corresponding detection ranges were 0.04–100% and 0.03–45%. For gaseous water detection, the sensitivity of humidity detection was as low as 23% and the fluorescence intensity increased linearly with increasing relative humidity from 23–85%. The sensitivity of CDs@MIL-101 was superior to other similar sensors and bare CDs for water detection. Intriguingly, CDs@MIL-101 exhibited FL color-visual resolution by the naked eye from blue to cyan in the presence of 0.2% water content. Moreover, CDs@MIL-101 exhibited excellent reusable stability, whose skeleton structure and fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged after five cycles. This outstanding water detection performance was ascribed to adsorption-solvent synergistic mechanism of high water capture and sensitive changes of fluorescence emission intensity and wavelength from confined CDs within MIL-101. DFT theoretical simulation was carried out and the result suggested that water molecules exhibited merits of diffusion and selective adsorption compared to tetrahydrofuran and acetone in MIL-101(Cr), which can effectively capture H2O molecules from solvents and air. Meanwhile, CDs with unique surface properties not only captured water molecules under trace concentration, but also responded to solvent molecules sensitively. Thus, we obtained high sensitivity in both liquid and gas conditions. Additionally, we found that the overlap rate of excitation wavelengths for water and other solvents showed mathematic relation to the relative polarity and hydrogen donation of the solvent, which can help us to rapidly predict the detection conditions (i.e., excitation wavelength) and sensitivity in unknown solvents with known polarity and hydrogen-bonding donor acidity. Such host-guest systems and multiphase fluorescence sensor will offer the guidance for optimizing and expanding the detection performance of CDs, which was of great significance in the design and construction of novel multiphase sensors and even the development of new materials.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wen-Yuan Li is a master's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His current research interests are the preparation and application of MOFs and carbon quantum dots fluorescent composites.

Xiao-Ting Yu is a master's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. Her current research interests are the preparation and application of carbon quantum dots fluorescent probes.
Ying Tang is a master's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His current research interests are the preparation and application of biomimetic enzymes.
Syed Jalil Shah is a Ph.D. candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His main research direction is the preparation and catalytic performance of MOFs composites.

Yu-Ying Liu is a Ph.D. candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zian Li. Her current research interests are the development and application of new techniques for transmission electron microscopy of functional material structure and physical properties.

Han-Qing Zhao is a undergraduate's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His research direction is the preparation and application of carbon quantum dots fluorescent probes.
Ming-Xin Song is a undergraduate's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His research direction is the preparation of carbon quantum dots fluorescent probes.
Jun-jie Li is a master's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His research direction is the preparation and properties of MOFs composites.
Guan-Chen Wang is a undergraduate's candidate at Guangxi University under the supervision of Prof. Zhenxia Zhao. His research direction is the preparation of carbon quantum dots fluorescent probes.
Li-qin Zhou, received her PhD (2021) in chemical engineering from Guangxi University，and basic research in green chemical industry and renewable resources.

Zhong-Xing Zhao. received his PhD (2012) from Guangxi University. He visited Tsinghua University from 2019 to 2020. He is now a Professor at Guangxi University. His main scientific interests are the application of nano-enzyme/bio-enzyme in the fields of highly sensitive detection of biological activity and bionic catalysis.
Suya Liu, technical staff of Shanghai Thermo Fisher Technology Co., LTD., mainly studies the development and application of new technologies such as transmission electron microscopy.

Zhen-Xia Zhao, received her PhD (2009) in energy and environmental engineering from South China University of Technology, She visited Arizona State University from 2007 to 2009, She did postdoctoral fellowship at South China University in 2010. She is now a Professor at Guangxi University (GXU), and her research interests focus on micro- and nano- design of porous materials such as metal-organic frameworks and carbon materials, and basic research in the direction of detection, environment and energy.
